medications.indication_rationale: Regular simple analgesia is the backbone of acute low back pain management, enabling the early mobilisation that shortens recovery. Given orally at the dose used in the source encounter.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally, up to four times daily (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of an oral NSAID reduces musculoligamentous inflammation and pain and is guideline first-line for acute low back pain in patients without contraindications. Dose as given in the source encounter.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally, three times daily with food, short course
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension, or NSAID allergy before advising — none reported, but not examinable via chat; take with food and use the shortest effective course.

medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician prescribed a short, strictly time-limited course to break the acute paralysing spasm. A benzodiazepine muscle relaxant is a reasonable short-term adjunct for severe spasm but is prescription-only, sedating and dependence-forming, so it is second-line to simple analgesia and cannot be initiated by the AI Doctor — it requires a prescriber. Flagged rather than directed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 mg orally, up to twice daily, for a maximum of 3 days (as prescribed in the source encounter)
contraindications_in_this_case: Patient lives alone and is currently very sedated by pain — sedation and fall/over-sedation risk; must not drive; avoid with alcohol or other CNS depressants; limit to the shortest course.

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are not indicated for acute non-specific low back pain — they provide little functional benefit over simple analgesia and carry sedation, constipation, falls and dependence risk. Reaching for an opioid here is an over-prescribing error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid strict bed rest. Once the acute spasm eases with analgesia, begin short, gentle movement — even 5 minutes of walking around the house every couple of hours from the next morning — and gradually increase activity as tolerated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early gentle mobilisation flushes inflammatory mediators, prevents deconditioning, and shortens recovery; prolonged bed rest weakens supporting muscles and prolongs the spasm.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a heat pack to the lower back for about 20 minutes every couple of hours, and avoid lifting anything heavier than a cup of tea for the next 72 hours.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Local heat relaxes the spasm and increases blood flow; temporary load reduction avoids re-triggering the spasm while it settles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once mobile, progress to a structured core-strengthening and general mobility program, ideally with a physiotherapist.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Improves paraspinal support and reduces recurrence of mechanical low back pain.

patient_education_points.point: Your back muscles have gone into a strong protective cramp/spasm that has locked your spine — this is painful and dramatic but it is not dangerous and it is not a slipped disc or a trapped nerve, because your legs, bladder and bowel are all working normally.
The pain-relief medicines settle the inflammation and un-clench the muscle so you can start moving; movement itself is part of the treatment, not something to avoid.
The intense spasm usually peaks over the next 24–48 hours then softens; the dull ache can linger 2–4 weeks but your ability to move returns much sooner.
X-rays and scans are not needed for this kind of back pain when there are no warning signs — they don't show muscle spasm and don't change the treatment.

follow_up_plan.interval: review with a GP or physiotherapist in 3–5 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: pain and function, response to analgesia, return of mobility, and to commence a structured core-strengthening and mobility program; re-screen for any interval red-flag features
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: pain not improving at all after 3–5 days, new radiation of pain below the knee, new leg weakness or numbness, or any bladder/bowel change (the last is an emergency, not a routine review)

must_include_items: Recognise this as benign acute non-specific mechanical low back pain / muscle spasm
Actively screen the cauda equina red flags by history (bladder/bowel control, urinary retention, saddle numbness, progressive leg weakness)
Recommend regular simple analgesia (paracetamol +/- a short NSAID course) rather than opioids
Advise against strict bed rest and encourage early gentle mobilisation
Give explicit cauda equina safety-netting with a 000/ED instruction if those features develop
Address the practical problem of being immobile and alone (arrange assistance to get off the floor safely)
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending paracetamol alone, or an NSAID alone, is acceptable first-line analgesia.
A short course of a muscle relaxant is an acceptable prescriber-initiated adjunct but is not required and cannot be supplied by the AI Doctor.
Advising a heat pack and topical analgesia as adjuncts is acceptable.
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for cauda equina red flags
Failing to provide the bladder/bowel/saddle-numbness safety-net
Failing to advise mobilisation (leaving the patient on bed rest)
Ignoring the social/functional problem of an immobile person living alone
errors_of_commission: Ordering lumbar imaging (X-ray/CT/MRI) with a negative red-flag screen
Prescribing or recommending opioids as first-line
Over-triaging a benign, red-flag-negative presentation to a 000 ambulance or ED as a medical emergency
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) a benign working diagnosis reached only AFTER a documented cauda equina/red-flag history screen, (b) conservative management (simple analgesia + mobilisation, not opioids or bed rest), and (c) explicit cauda equina safety-netting. Over-triage to emergency services, or omission of the cauda equina safety-net, are the key failure modes.
